- My solution is to use ssh-to-age to create keys from the ssh host key on each machine as part of initial deployment. Each host has its own directory of host-specific secrets, along with access to the common secrets directory. Like so:
    
    ```
    # .sops.yaml is @generated by make.
    keys:
      users:
        user: &user age1t63uca22rn9p2nz3tu3rvpnts5umzjvveuj78usq6mz6senrdafq2s6xfp
      hosts:
        draugr: &draugr age1s2j3exc9f3jsmqkdrpmdpy5vvnt49xzuk0z5504wyk67mfecteyq7umlum
        goblin-wsl: &goblin-wsl age1s4fzhq0gt9feswrvdn8q5ey0apja37pxp9j6e2d2trz925quzgjsxzek77
    [...]
    creation_rules:                                                             
      - path_regex: secrets/common/.*
        key_groups:                                                                     
          - age:                                                                  
              - *user                                                                     
              - *draugr                                                         
              - *goblin-wsl                                                               
             [...]                                               
      - path_regex: secrets/users/.*                                                      
        key_groups:                                                                   
          - age:                                                                  
              - *user                                                           
      - path_regex: secrets/draugr/.*
        key_groups:  
          - age:       
              - *draugr             
              - *user  
      - path_regex: secrets/goblin-wsl/.*
        key_groups:             
          - age:       
              - *goblin-wsl      
              - *user 
    ```
    
    (edited)
    

1. Host specific stuff like tailscale auth keys, back up auth tokens, etc obviously go in the host specific secrets.yaml files. common things are infra-wide, things like the mounting credentials for the shared NAS mounts and user login password hashes. Then user secrets are stuff specifc to the user accounts; github api tokens, aws access keys, etc.
    
2. My solution is to use ssh-to-age to create keys from the ssh host key on each machine as part of initial deployment. Each host has its own directory of host-specific secrets, along with access to the common secrets directory. Like so: # .sops.yaml is @generated by make. keys: users: user: &user age1t63uca22rn9p2nz3tu3rvpnts5umzjvveuj78usq6mz6senrdafq2s6xfp hosts: draugr: &draugr age1s2j3exc9f3jsmqkdrpmdpy5vvnt49xzuk0z5504wyk67mfecteyq7umlum goblin-wsl: &goblin-wsl age1s4fzhq0gt9feswrvdn8q5ey0apja37pxp9j6e2d2trz925quzgjsxzek77 [...] creation_rules: - path_regex: secrets/common/.* key_groups: - age: - *user - *draugr - *goblin-wsl [...] - path_regex: secrets/users/.* key_groups: - age: - *user - path_regex: secrets/draugr/.* key_groups: - age: - *draugr - *user - path_regex: secrets/goblin-wsl/.* key_groups: - age: - *goblin-wsl - *user ​ (edited)
    
    I... uh... like make a bit more than average. When I provision a new machine, that host gets a sops.yaml fragment which contains just it's key (as generated from the ssh key) and the rule for its host-specific files. This looks something like:
    
    ```
    keys:
      hosts: 
        skeleton: age1yf2hjx0lvwfr9gjdeadbeefvh3vh949jg4tu977sscc496dry2wj4jsw6hp55
    
    creation_rules: [ { path_regex: "secrets/skeleton/.*", key_groups: [{age: [skeleton, user, sopsmaster]}] } ]
    ```
    
    I then have a yq script which can take multiple inputs in this format and stitch them together so that I don't have to manually add each machine to the hosts lists and creation_rules.
    
    ds
    
    ```
     .sops.yaml: secrets/templates/sops.base.yaml secrets/*/sops.yaml ## rebuild .sops.yaml with all current hosts
    > yq ea --prettyPrint --from-file scripts/sops-merge.yq $^ > $@
    
    .PRECIOUS: secrets/%/sops.yaml
    secrets/%/sops.yaml: secrets/%/ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub
    > key=$$(ssh-to-age < $<)
    > host=$(@D)
    > host=$${host##*/}
    > sed -e "s/\$$host/$${host}/g" -e "s/\$$key/$${key}/g" < secrets/templates/sops.host.yaml > $@
    ```
    
    (tabs represented by >​) everything related to a host's secret material ends up in one directory:
    
    ```
    secrets/
    [...]
    ├── common
    │   └── secrets.yaml
    ├── draugr
    │   ├── secrets.yaml
    │   ├── sops.yaml
    │   ├── ssh_host_ed25519_key
    │   └── ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub
    [...]
    ```
    
    The ssh keys (sops encrypted in the case of the private key), the resulting age key in the sops.yaml fragment, and then the actual host secrets (encrypted with the age key). Since the main sops.yaml is stitched out of all the host sops.yaml fragments, common/secrets.yaml can get re-encrypted with access for each host as they're provisioned, as well. advantage to me is that everything related to a host's secret material ends up in one directory: secrets/ [...] ├── common │ └── secrets.yaml ├── draugr │ ├── secrets.yaml │ ├── sops.yaml │ ├── ssh_host_ed25519_key │ └── ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub [...]​ The ssh keys (sops encrypted in the case of the private key), the resulting age key in the sops.yaml fragment, and then the actual host secrets (encrypted with the age key). Since the main sops.yaml is stitched out of all the host sops.yaml fragments, common/secrets.yaml can get re-encrypted with access for each host as they're provisioned, as well.
    
    that is very cool, if a little overengineered for one or two machines, yeah. But that started growing around the time I hit 5 or 6 active nix installs, and I'm currently sitting at 8, so a little automation helps a lot.
